prompt: Which organ is the largest in the human body?
How many bones does an adult human body have?
Which of these is the smallest bone in your body?
Your heart is roughly the size of which everyday object?
Babies are born with around 270 bones, but adults only have 206. What happened to the rest?
options: They are shed like baby teeth

prompt: Your eye has a small 'blind spot' where you can't see anything. Why?
options: The cornea is thicker in that area
The pupil can't dilate wide enough
There are no photoreceptors where the optic nerve connects

prompt: The human liver is unique among organs — what can it do that almost no other organ can?
options: Regenerate itself even after 80% is removed
Store enough energy for 30 days without food
Function for up to a week outside the body

prompt: By the time you reach age 70, roughly how much dead skin will your body have shed over your lifetime?
Your tongue has thousands of taste buds — but taste buds also appear somewhere else on the body. Where?
options: On the epiglottis and soft palate

prompt: Which part of the human body has no blood supply at all and gets oxygen directly from the air?

What is the hard, white outer covering of a tooth called?
Which organs fill with air every time you take a breath?
What is the colored ring around the dark pupil of your eye called?

What do we call the wave of muscle squeezing that pushes food down from your throat to your stomach?
Which organ has the most sweat glands, helping cool you down when you're hot?
Roughly how many times does an average human heart beat in a single day?
Why do your fingers wrinkle up after a long soak in the bath?
options: The skin soaks up water and swells
Your nervous system shrinks blood vessels under the skin
Salt is pulled out of the skin
Blood drains away from your hands

prompt: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve metal. Why doesn't it digest your own stomach?
options: A layer of mucus constantly protects the stomach lining
The acid only activates when food arrives
The stomach has no living cells to harm
The acid is neutralized as soon as it forms

prompt: Which gland, about the size of a pea at the base of the brain, is often called the body's 'master gland'?
Goosebumps are left over from our furry ancestors. What actually causes that bumpy skin?
options: Blood rushing to the skin's surface
Tiny muscles tugging each hair upright

prompt: Your 'funny bone' isn't a bone at all. What do you actually hit when your elbow tingles painfully?
Which two senses are so closely linked that a blocked nose makes food taste bland?
Bone is famously tough. Weight for weight, how does its strength roughly compare to steel?
options: Bone is about as strong as steel but much lighter
Bone is far weaker than steel
Bone is exactly equal to steel in every way
Bone is ten times stronger than any metal

prompt: The femur and other large bones aren't just structural — their inner marrow has a vital job. What is it?
options: Generating most of the body's heat
Storing the body's main fat reserves

prompt: Which organ can keep beating for a short time even after being removed from the body, thanks to its own electrical pacemaker?
The vagus nerve is one of the body's most far-reaching nerves. Which best describes where it goes?
options: It serves only the muscles of the face
It runs only within the spinal cord
It wanders from the brainstem down to the heart, lungs, and gut
It connects just the two halves of the brain

prompt: Roughly how many cells make up an adult human body, by current scientific estimates?

What carries instructions from your brain to your muscles, telling them when to move?
Which organs filter waste out of your blood and turn it into urine?
What is the watery liquid in your mouth called, which starts breaking down food before you even swallow?
When the doctor taps just below your kneecap and your leg kicks out by itself, what is this an example of?
Even identical twins have different fingerprints. When do your fingerprint patterns actually form?
options: In the first year after birth
Only once you start using your hands

prompt: If you laid out all the blood vessels in one adult body end to end, roughly how far would they stretch?

What actually triggers the urge to breathe — the thing your body is most desperate to get rid of?

options: At the base of the skull

prompt: You are measurably taller at one point in the day than another. When are you at your tallest?
options: In the morning, just after waking
There is no daily change at all
In the evening, after a day upright

prompt: Which fluid, made by the liver and stored in the gallbladder, helps your body break down fatty foods?

options: The only bone that never breaks
The only bone with no marrow

prompt: Adrenaline (epinephrine) floods your body in a fright. Which small glands, perched on top of your kidneys, release it?
